author: @bogey6925VR
text: Great idea but that bearing looks (and sounds) to be bad. Probably why it was thrown out in the first place. I'm a washer repair tech. Sometimes you can pop the cover out of the bearing to expose the little ball bearings and grease them, then pop the cover back in. Just a suggestion.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text: oh, and brilliant as usual.  I wonder if that design would work as a waterwheel?  I mean being a washing machine, all the bits are water Proof (bar the motor.)  That might work in a small stream for more consistent generation than wind, if one were near a decent source with a fair flow.  just a thought with the motor being a bit stiff to turn.

author: @ryanjamesloyd6733
text:
those fridges-  I'm wondering if one could turn a fridge into a stirling engine?  like, the refrigeration cycle is so efficient at moving heat around, just by virtue of how they work,  it seems like you could either integrate, or greatly improve the efficiency of a stirling engine and get useful work out of it beyond just refrigeration.  Pondering it hard.  I've got a janky AC unit, and an old mini fridge, but I'm not sure how to proceed.  Seems like a couple ways to do it- one being to segregate the hot and cold sides of the coil on the hot and cold pistons of the engine, well insulated (so basically just extracting mechanical work from the efficiency of a heat pump- which is greater than that of burned fuel or resistive heating by a Lot as I currently understand it)
OR use the refrigerant concept as the working fluid of the engine,  and exploiting heat pump high and low pressure geometry to more efficiently move the heat in the system.   I dunno.  That's been burning up my brain for a week, so if the idea struck you as worth exploring a vid on such a thing would be interesting.   seems like it might make a "more bang for your buck generator". 

In any case, I'd be interested to see what you could do with an old fridge, if you were of a mind to do so.  (I love your "found and broken" model.  I use the same for making jewelry, out of found and broken bits so it's a notion that's dear to me)
